systemsMapSummary: The target behaviour is gated by three reinforcing loops: a friction loop (each extra step at the moment of action compounds drop-off), a salience loop (the behaviour is easy to forget because nothing in the environment cues it), and a social-proof loop (people copy the visible norm, which currently runs against the desired behaviour). The highest-leverage points sit where these loops intersect — early in the journey, before habits set.

topLeveragePoints.point: Remove the highest-friction step at the moment of action
topLeveragePoints.currentState: A required step adds ~2 minutes and a context switch right when motivation peaks, causing most of the first-action drop-off.

topLeveragePoints.point: Add a timely environmental cue tied to an existing routine
topLeveragePoints.currentState: There is no reminder anchored to a moment the group already pays attention to, so the behaviour relies on memory.

topLeveragePoints.point: Make the desired behaviour the visible social norm
topLeveragePoints.currentState: Peers only see the old behaviour; the new one is invisible, so social proof pulls the wrong way.

headline: Three changes — cut one friction point, add one cue, flip the visible norm — plausibly move adoption from 31% toward the 50% target without new incentives.

comb.barrier: The action requires a step many in the group can't complete in the moment (tooling/context).
comb.evidence: Drop-off concentrates at the step that demands the context switch.

comb.barrier: People are unsure whether they did it 'right', so they hesitate to repeat.
comb.evidence: Repeat-action rate lags first-action rate by a wide margin.

comb.barrier: No environmental cue at the decision point; the behaviour depends on memory.
comb.evidence: Behaviour clusters right after any incidental reminder, then decays.

comb.barrier: The desired behaviour is invisible to peers, so the norm signals the opposite.
comb.evidence: Adoption is higher in sub-groups where the behaviour is publicly visible.

comb.barrier: Belief that the outcome is worth it is present but fragile under friction.
comb.evidence: Stated intent is high; behaviour falls off only when effort rises.

comb.barrier: No habit loop yet; nothing makes the behaviour feel automatic or rewarding.
comb.evidence: Week-8 sustained rate is unmeasured but anecdotally low.

barrierNarratives.point: Remove the highest-friction step at the moment of action
barrierNarratives.narrative: Effort at the decision point is the dominant barrier. Because motivation is already high, lowering friction converts existing intent directly into action — the cheapest, fastest lever. Pre-fill, defer, or remove the blocking step so the first action takes seconds, not minutes.

barrierNarratives.point: Add a timely environmental cue tied to an existing routine
barrierNarratives.narrative: Even motivated people forget. Anchoring a cue to a routine the group already performs turns memory-dependence into reliable triggering, addressing the Opportunity–Physical barrier without adding incentives.

barrierNarratives.point: Make the desired behaviour the visible social norm
barrierNarratives.narrative: Behaviour spreads by imitation. Making the new behaviour visible — counts, peer signals, defaults framed as 'most people' — flips social proof from a headwind into a tailwind, reinforcing the other two levers over time.

gapLog: Instrument the week-8 sustained-behaviour measure before launch.
Assign single-team ownership of the friction step to avoid split accountability.
Segment the group by capability vs. motivation barrier to target interventions.
Validate that the chosen cue routine actually has the group's attention.
discoveryActivities: 5–8 contextual interviews at the moment of action to confirm the friction step.
A friction audit / journey walkthrough timing each step to the decision point.
A norms survey to measure perceived vs. actual behaviour in the group.
A baseline instrumentation sprint to capture the week-8 sustained metric.
